memos.markdown:
# SK Hynix Crosses the Pacific: A $26.5B Nasdaq Listing Anchors the AI Memory Trade

The week's defining event was SK Hynix's $26.5 billion Nasdaq listing—the largest ever for a Korean semiconductor firm—arriving alongside macro data that validated the demand thesis behind it: Korean semiconductor exports surged 193% year-over-year in the first ten days of July, lifting total exports to a record $29.8 billion. Yet the equity market response was ambivalent. Shares fell 3.7–4.4% post-debut and slid another 6% intraday in Seoul, even as the U.S.-listed market cap surpassed Micron's. The dispersion between record fundamentals and cautious price action is this week's real story, and it should frame how corporate development teams read the AI supply chain from here.

## Deal activity

Dealmaking clustered squarely around AI infrastructure and semiconductor materials. The largest disclosed transaction was Element Solutions' agreed $14.5 billion acquisition of Solutics Advanced Materials, a specialty-materials play aimed directly at the chip supply chain. Anthropic's reported $19 billion arrangement with TeraWulf—structured with a $3.5 billion upfront component—drew scrutiny on financing adequacy, a reminder that compute-and-power commitments are outrunning conventional balance-sheet capacity.

The pattern beneath the headlines is vertical integration toward power and materials rather than horizontal consolidation. EQT is acquiring Copia Power, an integrated energy-and-digital-infrastructure platform; Nvidia and others are eyeing minority stakes in Lancium, the power backbone for Stargate; and a $4 billion Ohio data center project sits on hold pending hyperscaler commitment. Meta's reported $6.5 billion cloud-and-AI capacity push, target undisclosed, fits the same logic. On the materials side, Micron's $500 million minority investment in GlobalWafers is a supply-security move, not an acquisition. Domestically in Korea, the ~$580 million Nextflex sale stalled—final bids were delayed with no buyer named, a small but telling sign of cautious mid-market appetite. Government intervention also surfaced twice: a reported U.S. 10% stake in Intel and discussion of a 5% federal position in OpenAI.

## Capital markets

SK Hynix dominated the primary calendar. Beyond the $26.5 billion Nasdaq debut, the company filed a 424B4 prospectus supplement with the SEC, signaling a follow-on equity pricing event—confirmation it intends to keep tapping U.S. capital markets. The listing's mechanics rippled into FX: the ADR-driven dollar supply contributed to won weakness, with USD/KRW holding near 1,500.

Two smaller listings rounded out the week. General Fusion completed its de-SPAC combination with Spring Valley Acquisition Corp. III, becoming the first publicly listed fusion energy company on Nasdaq. Separately, KakaoPay Securities secured a dealer license, opening a path into IPO underwriting and bond origination. B Capital's read on India's deepening markets providing stable tech-IPO conditions is worth noting for regional exit planning.

## What we're watching

First, SK Hynix's pending follow-on: with the 424B4 on file, pricing terms and dilution impact will shape sentiment, especially against Korea Investment's flag that Q2 profit may miss consensus. The gap between record exports and a Q2 miss is the tension to resolve.

Second, the concentration risk. TSMC, Samsung, and SK Hynix now exceed 30% of the MSCI Emerging Markets index—a level that constrains incremental institutional buying and raises the stakes on any single-name disappointment.

Third, Samsung's foundry momentum: the reported tape-out of Tesla's AI5 chip on 2nm, plus Google Cloud's Gemini Enterprise rollout to Samsung's DX division, positions Samsung as both a logic supplier and an enterprise-AI reference customer worth tracking against OpenAI.

**Bottom line:** The AI memory trade is now a public-market franchise, but SK Hynix's soft debut warns that record fundamentals no longer guarantee a valuation premium.

# The Memory Cycle Comes to Nasdaq

SK Hynix's landmark $26.5 billion Nasdaq listing anchored a week defined by the collision of two forces: a record semiconductor demand cycle and equity markets already nervous about how much of it is priced in. Korean exports hit an all-time high of $29.8 billion in the first ten days of July, with semiconductors up 193% year-over-year to $11.2 billion — the clearest hard-data confirmation yet of the AI hardware boom. Yet SK Hynix shares fell 3.7% to 4.4% after the debut, and Samsung, SK Hynix, and TSMC now exceed 30% of the MSCI Emerging Markets index, a concentration that is starting to make institutional allocators uncomfortable even as the fundamentals hold.

## Deal activity

M&A this week clustered squarely around AI infrastructure and the semiconductor supply chain, not consumer or software targets. The two largest disclosed transactions were **Element Solutions' $14.5 billion acquisition of Solutics Advanced Materials**, a specialty-materials play aimed directly at the chip supply chain, and **Anthropic's reported $19 billion arrangement with TeraWulf**, structured with a $3.5 billion upfront payment — a structure already drawing scrutiny over financing adequacy.

The rest of the pipeline reads as a map of the AI power and compute buildout. **EQT is acquiring Copia Power**, an integrated energy-and-digital-infrastructure platform; **Nvidia is eyeing a minority stake in Lancium**, the power backbone for Stargate; and **Meta is pursuing a $6.5 billion cloud and AI capacity expansion** with an undisclosed target. Supply-chain security drove **Micron's $500 million strategic minority investment in GlobalWafers**. Government involvement is the week's quieter theme: reports of the **US acquiring a 10% stake in Intel** signal that semiconductor policy is now expressed through equity.

On the Korean side, the **Nextflex final-bid process slipped**, clouding an expected ~$580 million sale with no buyers named — a reminder that mid-market appetite remains selective even in a hot sector.

## Capital markets

SK Hynix dominated. The company **priced a $26.5 billion Nasdaq offering** via a 424B4 supplement filed July 10, the largest capital-markets event for a Korean chipmaker and one that pushed its US-listed market cap above Micron's. The mechanics rippled beyond equity: the ADR-driven dollar supply contributed to won weakness, with USD/KRW holding near 1,500. The post-debut selloff, alongside Korea Investment's warning that Q2 profit will miss consensus, suggests the listing priced closer to peak sentiment than to trough.

Elsewhere, **General Fusion completed its de-SPAC** with Spring Valley III, becoming the first publicly listed fusion company. **KakaoPay Securities obtained a dealer license**, opening IPO underwriting and bond lines — a structural entrant to watch in Korean capital markets.

## What we're watching

First, SK Hynix Q2 earnings. Korea Investment's below-consensus call, set against KB Securities' bullish 2027 "memory supply cliff" thesis, frames a genuine dispute over whether we are at a cycle peak or the front edge of a supercycle — the print will move the whole complex given its index weight.

Second, Samsung's foundry momentum. The reported completion of Tesla's AI5 tape-out on Samsung's 2nm node is a meaningful competitive marker against TSMC, which is simultaneously building three new packaging plants to ease AI bottlenecks. Confirmation of mass-production readiness would reset Samsung's foundry narrative.

Third, the Intel stake and the Nextflex bid. Both are pending, and both test different things — whether Washington's chip equity intervention proceeds, and whether Korean mid-market M&A can clear.

Bottom line: the demand data is unambiguously strong, but a $26.5 billion listing that immediately sold off tells you the market is pricing perfection, not upside.

# The Week the IPO Queue Became the Story

Three filings now sit in registration targeting a combined ~$3.5 trillion — SpaceX ($1.75T), Anthropic (~$965B), and OpenAI (~$852B) — an equity-supply test with no historical precedent. Against that backdrop, the week's announced M&A was modest in count but pointed in direction: chipmakers buying software.

## Deal activity

Qualcomm's ~$4B agreement for Modular is the week's signature transaction — the third case this cycle of silicon vendors acquiring the compiler/software layer rather than more silicon, aimed squarely at loosening the CUDA lock-in. Adobe's purchase of Topaz Labs (terms undisclosed) extends its pattern of buying model-adjacent imaging capability. Google/Wiz ($32B) remains in regulatory review with closing targeted for later this year.

## Capital markets

The convert market continues to reopen for AI infrastructure names: CoreWeave's 1.75% notes due 2031 traded above par this week, a meaningful signal after the winter selloff in neocloud credit. Quantinuum's June debut ($1.68B raised) continues to trade well, keeping the quantum window open. HBM supply reportedly sold out through 2027 shifts pricing power — and, eventually, capex financing needs — toward the memory complex.

## What we're watching

SpaceX float-size decisions, which will set allocation dynamics for the entire queue behind it; the Wiz closing timeline as the year's largest pending strategic deal; and TSMC's monthly sales print as the cleanest near-term read on AI accelerator demand.

Bottom line: supply of paper is about to test demand for the AI trade — and the acquirers who move before that repricing settle their currency questions first.

*(Example memo compiled from seed data as of Jul 6, 2026 — live memos begin with the first Friday run.)*
